project.name: ECC Rules
project.description: The layered coding-standards rule catalogue for Everything Claude Code (ECC): a language-agnostic common/ core of universal conventions (coding-style, git-workflow, testing, performance, patterns, hooks, agents, security) plus 20 language and domain-specific directories that extend and override it, where language-specific rules take precedence over the common defaults. Note: this project has over 100 source files; consider scoping analysis to a subdirectory for faster results.

nodes.name: coding-style.md
nodes.summary: Angular coding-style rules covering version awareness, file naming, standalone components, modern inject()-based dependency injection, signals (linkedSignal, resource, effect), templates, forms, component styles, and change detection.
nodes.languageNotes: Centers on modern Angular signal primitives (signal/computed/linkedSignal/resource/effect) and the inject() DI idiom over constructor injection.

nodes.name: hooks.md
nodes.summary: Angular hooks rule defining the PostToolUse and Stop hook integrations that enforce Angular tooling automation in the ECC harness.

nodes.name: patterns.md
nodes.summary: Angular architectural patterns covering smart/dumb component splits, the service layer, async data with resource, signal state, subscription cleanup, routing (guards, resolvers, view transitions), scoped DI, HTTP interceptors, RxJS operators, forms, rendering strategies, and accessibility.
nodes.languageNotes: Emphasizes functional route guards/resolvers, InjectionToken-based scoped providers, and viewProviders vs providers distinctions.

nodes.name: security.md
nodes.summary: Angular security rule mandating XSS prevention, HTTP security, secret management, route guards, SSR security, and Content Security Policy practices.

nodes.name: testing.md
nodes.summary: Angular testing rule covering the test runner, TestBed setup, signal inputs, component harnesses, router/async/HTTP/service testing, what to test, E2E testing, and coverage expectations.

nodes.name: coding-style.md
nodes.summary: HarmonyOS/ArkTS coding-style rule extending the common coding-style guide, covering ArkTS language constraints (strict typing, no any/unknown, no structural typing), naming, formatting, file organization, error handling, and immutability.
nodes.languageNotes: Documents ArkTS's stricter-than-TypeScript constraints: mandatory static typing, prohibited destructuring/spread in some forms, and restricted dynamic property access.

nodes.name: hooks.md
nodes.summary: HarmonyOS/ArkTS hooks rule extending the common hooks guide with HAP/DevEco build commands and recommended PreToolUse/PostToolUse automations for editing .ets/.ts files, module.json5, and oh-package.json5, including a V1 decorator guard.

nodes.name: patterns.md
nodes.summary: HarmonyOS/ArkTS patterns rule extending the common patterns guide, mandating V2-only state management decorators, Navigation-based routing, MVVM architecture, state-driven ArkUI animation, and LazyForEach/component-reuse performance patterns.
nodes.languageNotes: Enforces ArkUI V2 state decorators (@ComponentV2, @Local, @Param) and prohibits the legacy V1 decorator set; routing is restricted to the Navigation component over deprecated router APIs.

nodes.name: security.md
nodes.summary: HarmonyOS/ArkTS security rule extending the common security guide, covering module.json5 permission declaration and runtime requests, secret management, input validation, network/TLS security, encrypted data storage, and dependency security.

nodes.name: testing.md
nodes.summary: HarmonyOS/ArkTS testing rule extending the common testing guide, specifying the test framework, directory structure, run commands, unit and UI test examples, a TDD workflow, and coverage requirements with best practices.

nodes.name: agents.md
nodes.summary: Universal agent-orchestration principle defining the catalogue of specialized subagents and when to delegate, run them in parallel, or combine them for multi-perspective analysis.

nodes.name: code-review.md
nodes.summary: Universal code-review standard specifying mandatory review triggers, the review checklist, severity levels, and the workflow that gates code before it merges.

nodes.name: coding-style.md
nodes.summary: Universal coding-style principles — immutability, KISS/DRY/YAGNI, error handling, naming, and code smells to avoid — that every language ruleset specializes.

nodes.name: development-workflow.md
nodes.summary: Universal feature-implementation pipeline — research and reuse first, then plan, TDD, and review — that precedes the git operations described in git-workflow.md.

nodes.name: git-workflow.md
nodes.summary: Universal git conventions defining the conventional commit message format and the pull-request workflow used across all languages.

nodes.name: hooks.md
nodes.summary: Universal hooks-system guidance covering hook types (PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop), cautious auto-accept permissions, and TodoWrite best practices.

nodes.name: patterns.md
nodes.summary: Universal design-pattern guidance favoring battle-tested skeleton projects and standard patterns such as the Repository pattern and a consistent API response format.

nodes.name: performance.md
nodes.summary: Universal performance guidance covering model selection (Haiku/Sonnet/Opus), context-window management, extended thinking with plan mode, and build troubleshooting.

nodes.name: security.md
nodes.summary: Universal security baseline enumerating mandatory pre-commit checks, secret management rules, and the incident response protocol every language ruleset inherits.

nodes.name: testing.md
nodes.summary: Universal testing requirements mandating 80% coverage, three test tiers, the red-green-refactor TDD workflow, and the Arrange-Act-Assert test structure.

nodes.name: coding-style.md
nodes.summary: C++-specific coding-style rules layered on the common standard, covering modern C++17/20/23 idioms, RAII-based resource management, naming conventions, and clang-format formatting.
nodes.languageNotes: Lines 1-10 are a shared prompt-defense preamble; the rule body begins at the H1 on line 11.

nodes.name: hooks.md
nodes.summary: C++-specific build and CI hook rules extending the common hooks standard, defining build-time checks and a recommended CI pipeline for C++ projects.

nodes.name: patterns.md
nodes.summary: C++ design-pattern rules extending the common patterns standard, covering RAII, the Rule of Five/Zero, value semantics, and error-handling idioms.

nodes.name: security.md
nodes.summary: C++ security rules extending the common security standard, addressing memory safety, buffer overflows, undefined behavior, and static-analysis tooling.

nodes.name: testing.md
nodes.summary: C++ testing rules extending the common testing standard, covering the test framework, running tests, coverage measurement, and runtime sanitizers.

nodes.name: coding-style.md
nodes.summary: C#-specific coding-style rules extending the common layer, covering type/model conventions, immutability (records, init-only), async and error-handling patterns, and formatting standards.

nodes.name: hooks.md
nodes.summary: C#-specific hook rules extending the common layer, defining PostToolUse and Stop hook conventions for .NET tooling integration.

nodes.name: patterns.md
nodes.summary: C#-specific design-pattern rules extending the common layer, covering the API response, repository, and options patterns plus dependency injection conventions.

nodes.name: security.md
nodes.summary: C#-specific security rules extending the common layer, covering secret management, SQL-injection prevention, input validation, authentication/authorization, and secure error handling.

nodes.name: testing.md
nodes.summary: C#-specific testing rules extending the common layer, covering the test framework choice, test organization, ASP.NET Core integration tests, and coverage expectations.

nodes.name: coding-style.md
nodes.summary: Dart/Flutter coding-style rules covering formatting, immutability, naming, null safety, Dart 3 sealed types and pattern matching, error handling, async/futures, imports, and code generation. Extends the common coding-style rules with language-specific guidance.
nodes.languageNotes: Targets **/*.dart, pubspec.yaml, and analysis_options.yaml; emphasizes Dart 3+ sealed classes and exhaustive switch patterns.

nodes.name: hooks.md
nodes.summary: Dart/Flutter automation hooks defining PostToolUse formatting/analysis hooks, a recommended hook configuration, pre-commit checks, and useful one-liners. Extends the common hooks rules with Dart-specific tooling.

nodes.name: patterns.md
nodes.summary: Dart/Flutter architectural patterns covering the repository pattern, BLoC/Cubit and Riverpod state management, dependency injection, ViewModel and UseCase patterns, freezed immutable state, clean-architecture layer boundaries, and GoRouter navigation. Extends the common patterns rules.
nodes.languageNotes: Covers competing state-management approaches (BLoC/Cubit vs Riverpod) and enforces clean-architecture layer boundaries with freezed and GoRouter.

nodes.name: security.md
nodes.summary: Dart/Flutter security rules covering secrets management, network security, input validation, data protection, Android- and iOS-specific hardening, WebView security, and build-time obfuscation. Extends the common security rules with mobile-specific guidance.
nodes.languageNotes: Adds mobile-specific path targets (AndroidManifest.xml, Info.plist) and platform-specific hardening for Android and iOS.

nodes.name: testing.md
nodes.summary: Dart/Flutter testing rules covering the test framework and test types, unit testing of BLoC (bloc_test) and Riverpod (ProviderContainer) state managers, widget tests, fakes-over-mocks, async testing, golden tests, naming, organization, and coverage. Extends the common testing rules.
nodes.languageNotes: Prescribes fakes over mocks and pairs state-manager tests to their frameworks (bloc_test for BLoC, ProviderContainer for Riverpod).

nodes.name: coding-style.md
nodes.summary: F#-specific coding-style rule extending the common coding-style guidance, covering type/model design, immutability, function style, async and error handling, and formatting conventions including open-declaration order.
nodes.languageNotes: Scoped via frontmatter glob paths (**/*.fs, **/*.fsx); emphasizes F# idioms like records, discriminated unions, and pipeline-friendly function style.

nodes.name: hooks.md
nodes.summary: F#-specific hooks rule extending the common hooks guidance, defining PostToolUse and Stop hook conventions for F# project and solution files.
nodes.languageNotes: Frontmatter scopes the rule broadly across F# build files (*.fsproj, *.sln, *.slnx, Directory.Build.props/targets) in addition to source files.

nodes.name: patterns.md
nodes.summary: F#-specific design-patterns rule extending the common patterns guidance, covering Result-based error handling, Option for missing values, discriminated unions for domain modeling, computation expressions, module organization, and dependency injection.
nodes.languageNotes: Centers on functional idioms — Result/Option monadic flows and computation expressions — rather than OO patterns.

nodes.name: security.md
nodes.summary: F#-specific security rule extending the common security guidance, covering secret management, SQL injection prevention, input validation, authentication/authorization, and secure error handling for F# and ASP.NET Core projects.
nodes.languageNotes: Frontmatter extends scope to appsettings*.json, tying secret-management rules to .NET configuration files.

nodes.name: testing.md
nodes.summary: F#-specific testing rule extending the common testing guidance, covering the test framework choice, test organization, property-based testing with FsCheck, ASP.NET Core integration tests, and coverage expectations.
nodes.languageNotes: Includes FsCheck property-based testing, an idiom particularly well-suited to F#'s functional, pure-function style.

nodes.name: coding-style.md
nodes.summary: Go-specific coding-style rules layered over common/coding-style.md, mandating gofmt/goimports and the 'accept interfaces, return structs' design override plus %w error wrapping. Overrides the common immutability/style defaults with Go idioms (small 1-3 method interfaces, struct returns) rather than the language-agnostic baseline.
nodes.languageNotes: Deliberate override example: Go favors returning concrete structs and accepting small interfaces, diverging from the common rule set's generic immutability/style guidance.

nodes.name: hooks.md
nodes.summary: Go-specific PostToolUse hook rules extending common/hooks.md, wiring gofmt/goimports auto-format, go vet, and staticcheck to run after editing .go files.

nodes.name: patterns.md
nodes.summary: Go design-pattern rules extending common/patterns.md, covering functional options, consumer-defined small interfaces, and constructor-based dependency injection with code examples.

nodes.name: security.md
nodes.summary: Go security rules extending common/security.md, covering env-var secret management, gosec static scanning, and context.Context timeout enforcement.

nodes.name: testing.md
nodes.summary: Go testing rules extending common/testing.md, prescribing table-driven go test, mandatory -race detection, and -cover coverage reporting.

nodes.name: coding-style.md
nodes.summary: Java-specific coding-style rules covering formatting, immutability (records, final fields, defensive copies), naming conventions, modern language features (sealed types, pattern matching, switch expressions), Optional usage, error handling, and streams. Extends the shared common/coding-style.md.
nodes.languageNotes: Emphasizes Java 14-21 features: records, sealed classes, pattern matching in instanceof and switch, and text blocks.

nodes.name: hooks.md
nodes.summary: Java-specific PostToolUse hook configuration guidance — auto-formatting with google-java-format, checkstyle style checks, and Maven/Gradle compile verification after edits. Extends the shared common/hooks.md.

nodes.name: patterns.md
nodes.summary: Java architectural pattern catalogue: repository pattern, service layer, constructor injection, DTO mapping with records, builder pattern, sealed types for domain models, and generic API response envelopes. Extends the shared common/patterns.md.
nodes.languageNotes: Leverages records for DTOs and sealed interfaces with exhaustive switch (Java 21+) for domain modeling.

nodes.name: security.md
nodes.summary: Java security hardening rules covering secrets management, SQL injection prevention via PreparedStatement, input validation, authentication/authorization, dependency CVE scanning, and safe error-message handling. Extends the shared common/security.md.

nodes.name: testing.md
nodes.summary: Java testing standards built on JUnit 5, AssertJ, Mockito, and Testcontainers — covering test organization, unit and parameterized test patterns, integration tests, naming conventions, and coverage targets. Extends the shared common/testing.md.
nodes.languageNotes: Standardizes on JUnit 5 + AssertJ + Mockito, with Testcontainers for real database integration tests.

nodes.name: coding-style.md
nodes.summary: Kotlin-specific coding-style rules covering formatting, immutability, naming, null safety, sealed types, extension and scope functions, and error handling. Extends the common coding-style baseline.

nodes.name: hooks.md
nodes.summary: Kotlin-specific hook rules defining PostToolUse automations (e.g. formatting/lint commands) layered on the common hooks baseline.

nodes.name: patterns.md
nodes.summary: Kotlin and Android/KMP architectural patterns including dependency injection, ViewModel, Repository, UseCase, expect/actual multiplatform, coroutine patterns, and DSL builders. Extends the common patterns baseline.

nodes.name: security.md
nodes.summary: Kotlin and Android/KMP security rules covering secrets management, network security, input validation, data protection, authentication, ProGuard/R8, and WebView hardening. Extends the common security baseline.

nodes.name: testing.md
nodes.summary: Kotlin and Android/KMP testing rules covering the test framework, Turbine ViewModel testing, fakes over mocks, coroutine testing, Ktor MockEngine, Room/SQLDelight testing, and naming/organization. Extends the common testing baseline.

nodes.name: coding-style.md
nodes.summary: Nuxt-specific coding-style rules covering directory layout, auto-imports discipline, compiler macros, config-file separation, and head/meta handling. Extends the common coding-style baseline.

nodes.name: hooks.md
nodes.summary: Nuxt-specific hook rules defining typecheck, lint, and format commands plus a suggested PostToolUse chain for automated quality gates. Extends the common hooks baseline.

nodes.name: patterns.md
nodes.summary: Nuxt-specific architectural patterns covering data-fetch selection, shared state, Nitro server routes, route middleware, and hydration-safe rendering. Extends the common patterns baseline.

nodes.name: security.md
nodes.summary: Nuxt-specific security rules covering runtimeConfig public/private separation, server-route input validation, SSR payload leakage, cookie/auth passthrough, and SSRF on server $fetch. Extends the common security baseline.

nodes.name: testing.md
nodes.summary: Nuxt-specific testing rules covering test setup, runtime helpers, E2E helpers, and guidance on what to test at which level. Extends the common testing baseline.

nodes.name: coding-style.md
nodes.summary: Perl-specific coding-style rules covering standards, immutability, formatting (perltidy), and linting (perlcritic), extending the common coding-style baseline.
nodes.languageNotes: Frontmatter scopes the rule to Perl source globs (.pl, .pm, .t, .psgi, .cgi).

nodes.name: hooks.md
nodes.summary: Perl-specific automation-hook rules describing PostToolUse hooks and warning handling, extending the common hooks baseline.

nodes.name: patterns.md
nodes.summary: Perl-specific design-pattern rules covering the repository pattern, DTOs/value objects, resource management, module interfaces, and dependency management, extending the common patterns baseline.

nodes.name: security.md
nodes.summary: Perl-specific security rules covering taint mode, input validation, file I/O, process execution, SQL-injection prevention, and security scanning, extending the common security baseline.
nodes.languageNotes: Emphasizes Perl taint mode (-T) as a first-class defense mechanism.

nodes.name: testing.md
nodes.summary: Perl-specific testing rules covering the test framework, runner (prove), coverage (Devel::Cover), mocking, and common pitfalls, extending the common testing baseline.

nodes.name: coding-style.md
nodes.summary: PHP-specific coding-style rules layered on the common coding-style baseline, covering PSR/standards adherence, immutability, formatting, import ordering, and error handling for PHP and composer.json files.
nodes.languageNotes: Rule file scoped via YAML frontmatter path globs (**/*.php, **/composer.json) and explicitly extends common/coding-style.md.

nodes.name: hooks.md
nodes.summary: PHP-specific hook guidance extending the common hooks rules, defining PostToolUse automation expectations (e.g. static analysis via PHPStan/Psalm) and warnings about hook usage.
nodes.languageNotes: Frontmatter targets PHP toolchain configs (phpstan.neon, psalm.xml) in addition to *.php and composer.json.

nodes.name: patterns.md
nodes.summary: PHP design-pattern rules extending the common patterns baseline, prescribing thin controllers with explicit services, DTOs/value objects, dependency injection, and clear architectural boundaries.
nodes.languageNotes: Emphasizes Laravel-flavored service/controller separation; scoped to *.php and composer.json.

nodes.name: security.md
nodes.summary: PHP-specific security rules extending the common security baseline, covering input/output sanitization, database safety (SQL injection avoidance), secret and dependency management, and auth/session safety.
nodes.languageNotes: Frontmatter also matches composer.lock for dependency-vulnerability scope.

nodes.name: testing.md
nodes.summary: PHP-specific testing rules extending the common testing baseline, specifying the test framework (PHPUnit), coverage expectations, test organization, and Inertia-related testing guidance.
nodes.languageNotes: Frontmatter targets phpunit.xml / phpunit.xml.dist; Inertia section signals a Laravel + Inertia.js stack.

nodes.name: coding-style.md
nodes.summary: Python-specific coding-style rules extending the common style ruleset, covering standards, immutability conventions, and formatting expectations for `.py`/`.pyi` files.

nodes.name: fastapi.md
nodes.summary: Framework-specific ruleset for FastAPI projects, prescribing app structure (create_app, thin routers, separate request/response schemas), async I/O discipline, dependency-injection patterns, response-model safety, CORS/JWT security, and dependency-override testing.
nodes.languageNotes: FastAPI/Pydantic conventions: Depends() for DI, async def for I/O endpoints, response_model to gate exposed fields.

nodes.name: hooks.md
nodes.summary: Python-specific hooks rules extending the common hooks ruleset, focused on PostToolUse hook behavior and associated warnings.

nodes.name: patterns.md
nodes.summary: Python-specific design-pattern rules extending the common patterns ruleset, covering Protocol-based duck typing, dataclasses as DTOs, and context managers/generators.

nodes.name: security.md
nodes.summary: Python-specific security rules extending the common security ruleset, covering secret management and security scanning practices.

nodes.name: testing.md
nodes.summary: Python-specific testing rules extending the common testing ruleset, covering the test framework choice, coverage expectations, and test organization conventions.

nodes.name: coding-style.md
nodes.summary: React-specific coding-style rules covering file extensions, naming, component shape, JSX conventions, the RSC server/client boundary, imports, hooks discipline, state, and per-component file layout. Extends the TypeScript and common coding-style rules.
nodes.languageNotes: Layered rule file: opens with a blockquote declaring it extends typescript/coding-style.md and common/coding-style.md with React-specific overrides.

nodes.name: hooks.md
nodes.summary: Rules for React hooks — Rules of Hooks, when not to reach for useEffect, dependency arrays, cleanup, when useMemo/useCallback are worth it, custom hooks, useRef/useState/useSyncExternalStore patterns, React 19 additions, and the stale-closure trap. Explicitly distinct from the Claude Code hooks runtime.
nodes.languageNotes: Filename collision-aware: a leading blockquote clarifies this is React hooks, not the repo's hooks/ runtime, and notes it extends typescript/patterns.md and common/patterns.md.

nodes.name: patterns.md
nodes.summary: React architectural patterns: container/presentational split, a state-location decision tree, the RSC server/client boundary, Suspense + error boundaries, forms (controlled/uncontrolled/libraries), data fetching, lists/keys, composition over inheritance, compound components, portals, and React 19 ref forwarding, with pointer sections for Next.js and React Native.
nodes.languageNotes: Extends typescript/patterns.md and common/patterns.md; defers Next.js and React Native specifics to pointer sections and references skills/react-patterns/SKILL.md.

nodes.name: security.md
nodes.summary: React security rules covering XSS via dangerouslySetInnerHTML, unsafe URL schemes, target=_blank/rel hardening, Server Action input validation, env-var secret exposure, auth, CSP, prototype pollution, SSR template injection, third-party components, and production source-map exposure.
nodes.languageNotes: Extends typescript/security.md and common/security.md; concrete vulnerability-by-vulnerability rules with agent-support guidance.

nodes.name: testing.md
nodes.summary: React testing rules built around Testing Library: library choice, behavior-over-implementation principle, query priority, user-event interaction, async assertions, MSW network mocking, avoiding component snapshots, test setup helpers, custom-hook testing, accessibility assertions, when to reach for Playwright/Cypress, coverage targets, and anti-patterns.
nodes.languageNotes: Extends typescript/testing.md and common/testing.md; references skills/react-testing/SKILL.md for end-to-end scaffolding.

nodes.name: coding-style.md
nodes.summary: Ruby and Rails coding-style rule extending the common style guide: targets Ruby 3.3+/YJIT, RuboCop (rubocop-rails-omakase), Rails naming and directory conventions, and specific-exception error handling.
nodes.languageNotes: Front-matter scopes the rule to **/*.rb, **/*.rake, Gemfile, *.gemspec, and config.ru paths.

nodes.name: hooks.md
nodes.summary: Ruby and Rails hook rule extending common hooks: prescribes PostToolUse tooling (RuboCop, Brakeman, tests, bundler-audit), commit-time warnings for debugger/raw-SQL/destructive migrations, and CI gate command suggestions.
nodes.languageNotes: Front-matter scopes the rule to Ruby sources, Gemfile/Gemfile.lock, and config/routes.rb.

nodes.name: patterns.md
nodes.summary: Ruby and Rails architectural-patterns rule extending common patterns: Rails-way-first MVC, when to extract service/query/form objects, PostgreSQL vs SQLite persistence, Solid Queue/Sidekiq jobs, Hotwire frontend, and Rails 8 vs Devise auth.
nodes.languageNotes: Front-matter scopes the rule to Ruby sources, Gemfile, app/**/*.erb, and config/routes.rb.

nodes.name: security.md
nodes.summary: Ruby and Rails security rule extending common security: CSRF and strong parameters, parameterized Active Record SQL, session rotation and account-recovery hardening, bundler-audit/Brakeman dependency checks, and template/upload/background-input web safety.
nodes.languageNotes: Front-matter scopes the rule to Ruby sources, Gemfile/Gemfile.lock, config/routes.rb, and encrypted credentials files.

nodes.name: testing.md
nodes.summary: Ruby and Rails testing rule extending common testing: Minitest vs RSpec framework choice, the test pyramid across model/request/system layers, fixtures vs factory_bot, project-local test commands, and SimpleCov coverage discipline.
nodes.languageNotes: Front-matter scopes the rule to Ruby sources, Gemfile, test/**, spec/**, and config/routes.rb.

nodes.name: coding-style.md
nodes.summary: Rust-specific coding-style rules layered on the common standard, covering rustfmt formatting, immutability, naming conventions, ownership/borrowing, idiomatic error handling, iterators over loops, and module organization and visibility.
nodes.languageNotes: Emphasizes Rust idioms: borrow-don't-clone, Result/? error propagation, and iterator chains over explicit loops.

nodes.name: hooks.md
nodes.summary: Short rule file defining Rust-specific automation hooks (PostToolUse) that extend the common hook standard, scoped to .rs and Cargo.toml files.

nodes.name: patterns.md
nodes.summary: Catalog of idiomatic Rust design patterns extending the common patterns standard, including trait-based repositories, service layers, the newtype pattern, enum state machines, builders, sealed traits, and API response envelopes.
nodes.languageNotes: Leans on Rust's trait system and type safety: newtypes, sealed traits for extensibility control, and enum-driven state machines.

nodes.name: security.md
nodes.summary: Rust-specific security guidance extending the common security standard, covering secrets management, SQL injection prevention, input validation, unsafe-code discipline, dependency auditing, and safe error messages.
nodes.languageNotes: Addresses Rust-unique concerns such as auditing unsafe blocks and vetting dependencies with cargo-audit.

nodes.name: testing.md
nodes.summary: Rust testing standard extending the common testing rules, covering the built-in test framework, test organization, unit and parameterized tests, async tests, mocking with mockall, naming conventions, coverage, and cargo test commands.
nodes.languageNotes: Covers Rust-specific tooling: #[cfg(test)] modules, #[tokio::test] async tests, mockall, and coverage via cargo-tarpaulin/llvm-cov.

nodes.name: coding-style.md
nodes.summary: Swift-specific coding-style rules extending the common style guide, covering formatting, immutability (let over var), API naming, typed error handling, and structured concurrency (async/await, actors).
nodes.languageNotes: Front-matter scopes the rule to **/*.swift and Package.swift; opens by extending common/coding-style.md.

nodes.name: hooks.md
nodes.summary: Swift-specific PostToolUse hook recommendations: auto-run SwiftFormat, SwiftLint, and swift build after edits, plus a warning to replace print() with os.Logger or structured logging.
nodes.languageNotes: Extends common/hooks.md; hooks configured in ~/.claude/settings.json.

nodes.name: patterns.md
nodes.summary: Swift design-pattern rules extending the common patterns guide, covering protocol-oriented design, value types (structs/enums), the actor concurrency pattern, and dependency injection.
nodes.languageNotes: Emphasizes Swift idioms — protocol-oriented programming, value semantics, and actors for safe shared mutable state.

nodes.name: security.md
nodes.summary: Swift-specific security rules extending the common security guide: Keychain-based secret management (never UserDefaults), App Transport Security and certificate pinning, and input validation for untrusted external data.
nodes.languageNotes: References iOS/Apple primitives — Keychain Services, ATS, ProcessInfo environment, and validated URL(string:).

nodes.name: testing.md
nodes.summary: Swift-specific testing rules extending the common testing guide, covering the Swift Testing framework, test isolation, parameterized tests, and coverage expectations.
nodes.languageNotes: Targets the modern Swift Testing framework (@Test / #expect) rather than legacy XCTest.

nodes.name: coding-style.md
nodes.summary: Foundational TypeScript/JavaScript coding-style ruleset covering explicit public-API typing, interfaces vs. type aliases, avoiding `any`, React props, immutability, error handling, Zod input validation, and a no-console.log rule. Acts as the base layer that the react, angular, vue, and nuxt rulesets extend.
nodes.languageNotes: Demonstrates idiomatic TypeScript patterns: `unknown` narrowing over `any`, string-literal unions over enums, `Readonly<T>` for immutable updates, and `z.infer` to derive types from Zod schemas.

nodes.name: hooks.md
nodes.summary: TypeScript/JavaScript hooks rules specifying PostToolUse automations (Prettier auto-format, `tsc` type check, console.log warning) and a Stop hook that audits modified files for console.log before a session ends.

nodes.name: patterns.md
nodes.summary: TypeScript/JavaScript design-pattern reference providing canonical generic shapes for API response envelopes, a custom React hook (useDebounce), and a generic Repository interface.
nodes.languageNotes: Centered on TypeScript generics (`ApiResponse<T>`, `useDebounce<T>`, `Repository<T>`) to express reusable, type-parameterized contracts.

nodes.name: security.md
nodes.summary: TypeScript/JavaScript security rules mandating environment-variable-based secret management (no hardcoded secrets) and pointing to the security-reviewer skill for comprehensive audits.

nodes.name: testing.md
nodes.summary: TypeScript/JavaScript testing rules selecting Playwright as the E2E framework for critical user flows and referencing the e2e-runner agent for Playwright testing support.

nodes.name: coding-style.md
nodes.summary: Vue-specific coding-style rules covering SFC structure, reactivity discipline, computed vs watchers, lifecycle/DOM handling, and compiler macros; extends the common coding-style baseline.

nodes.name: hooks.md
nodes.summary: Vue PostToolUse automation hook rules specifying vue-tsc typecheck, lint/format targets, architecture boundary checks, and hook sequencing; extends the common hooks baseline.

nodes.name: patterns.md
nodes.summary: Vue architectural pattern rules for composables, props/emits/v-model, provide/inject, Pinia stores, vue-router, and vue-query server caching; extends the common patterns baseline.

nodes.name: security.md
nodes.summary: Vue security rules on automatic escaping, trusted-template-only usage, v-html and render-function risks, URL/style/event injection, and avoiding client-bundle secrets; extends the common security baseline.

nodes.name: testing.md
nodes.summary: Vue testing rules covering the Vitest + Vue Test Utils stack, async rendering, what to test, composable and Pinia testing, and mount configuration; extends the common testing baseline.

nodes.name: coding-style.md
nodes.summary: Web-domain coding-style rules covering file organization, CSS custom properties, animation-only properties, semantic-HTML-first authoring, and naming conventions. Extends the common coding-style baseline with frontend-specific guidance.

nodes.name: design-quality.md
nodes.summary: Web design-quality standards enforcing an anti-template policy with banned patterns, required qualities, a pre-coding checklist, worthwhile style directions, and a per-component review checklist. Extends the common patterns baseline.

nodes.name: hooks.md
nodes.summary: Web-domain hook recommendations defining PostToolUse hooks (format-on-save, lint, type-check, CSS lint), a PreToolUse file-size guard, a Stop-hook final build verification, and hook ordering. Extends the common hooks baseline.

nodes.name: patterns.md
nodes.summary: Web architecture patterns spanning component composition (compound components, render props/slots, container/presentational split), state management, URL-as-state, and data fetching (stale-while-revalidate, optimistic updates, parallel loading). Extends the common patterns baseline.

nodes.name: performance.md
nodes.summary: Web performance rules setting Core Web Vitals targets and bundle budgets, plus loading strategy, image and font optimization, animation performance, and a performance checklist. Extends the common performance baseline.

nodes.name: security.md
nodes.summary: Web security rules covering Content Security Policy (nonce-based CSP), XSS prevention, third-party script handling, HTTPS and security headers, and form safety. Extends the common security baseline.

nodes.name: testing.md
nodes.summary: Web testing rules ordering priorities across visual regression, accessibility, performance, cross-browser, and responsive checks, plus E2E test shape and unit-test guidance. Extends the common testing baseline.

nodes.name: README.md
nodes.summary: Top-level documentation for the ECC rules catalogue, explaining the layered directory structure (a shared common/ core plus per-language directories), the two installation methods (install script and manual), the rules-vs-skills distinction, how to add a new language, and the rule-priority model where language-specific rules override common rules like CSS specificity.
nodes.languageNotes: Markdown catalogue index whose Rule Priority section frames the common-vs-language precedence model using a CSS-specificity analogy.

layers.name: Foundation: Universal Principles
layers.description: The language-agnostic core of the rule catalogue (common/) — agents, code-review, coding-style, development-workflow, git-workflow, hooks, patterns, performance, security, and testing — that every language ruleset inherits and overrides, like the base of a CSS-specificity cascade.

layers.name: TypeScript & Web Core
layers.description: The shared frontend substrate that sits between the universal core and the UI frameworks: the foundational TypeScript/JavaScript ruleset plus the web-domain rules (CSS, semantic HTML, design quality, Core Web Vitals) that react, angular, vue, and nuxt all build on.

layers.name: Frontend Frameworks
layers.description: Component-based UI framework rulesets — React, Angular, Vue, and Nuxt — that extend the TypeScript & Web Core layer with framework-specific component, state, routing, and rendering conventions.

layers.name: JVM Languages
layers.description: Rulesets for the JVM ecosystem — Java and Kotlin (including Android/KMP) — extending the universal core with records, sealed types, coroutines, and JVM-specific tooling and testing standards.

layers.name: .NET Languages
layers.description: Rulesets for the .NET ecosystem — C# and F# — extending the universal core with records/immutability, ASP.NET Core security, and framework-appropriate (xUnit, FsCheck property-based) testing conventions.

layers.name: Systems Languages
layers.description: Rulesets for compiled systems languages — Rust, C++, and Go — extending the universal core with ownership/RAII memory discipline, idiomatic error handling, static-analysis tooling, and race/sanitizer-aware testing.

layers.name: Scripting Languages
layers.description: Rulesets for dynamic scripting and web-backend languages — Python (including the FastAPI framework ruleset), Ruby/Rails, Perl, and PHP — extending the universal core with interpreter-specific style, taint/injection safety, and framework testing conventions.

layers.name: Mobile & Cross-Platform
layers.description: Rulesets for mobile and cross-platform app languages — Swift (Apple platforms), Dart/Flutter, and ArkTS/HarmonyOS — extending the universal core with platform-specific UI patterns, mobile security hardening, and widget/UI testing.

layers.name: Catalogue Index
layers.description: The catalogue's entry point — the top-level README that documents the layered rule hierarchy and points readers to the common core and per-language directories.

tour.title: The Catalogue Map
tour.description: Start at the top-level README, the catalogue's own explanation of itself. It lays out the layered directory structure (a shared common/ core plus one directory per language), the two install methods, the rules-vs-skills distinction, and the rule-priority model where language-specific rules override common rules like CSS specificity. Read this first and the rest of the tour becomes a guided walk through the structure it describes.
tour.languageLesson: These are 'rules' files — always-on guardrails written in Markdown with YAML frontmatter whose path globs scope each rule to the files it governs. Unlike on-demand skills, rules are loaded as standing context, so the catalogue is organized to keep each rule small and concern-focused.

tour.title: The Universal Core
tour.description: The common/ directory is the foundation every language inherits. These four files are the most depended-upon nodes in the entire catalogue: common/coding-style.md (immutability, KISS/DRY/YAGNI, naming), common/patterns.md (Repository pattern, consistent API responses), common/security.md (pre-commit checks, secret management, incident response), and common/testing.md (80% coverage, three test tiers, red-green-refactor TDD). Every per-language rule file points back to one of these as its baseline — understand them and you understand the default that all 20+ languages specialize.

tour.title: ECC's Working Philosophy
tour.description: Beyond the per-concern rules, common/ holds the meta-files that encode how ECC expects an agent to work. development-workflow.md defines the research-and-reuse-first, then plan-TDD-review pipeline; code-review.md gates code before merge with a checklist and severity levels; agents.md catalogues the specialized subagents and when to delegate or run them in parallel; hooks.md introduces the PreToolUse/PostToolUse/Stop automation model. Together these reveal the catalogue's agent-first, test-driven worldview — the 'why' behind the concern-specific rules in Step 2.

tour.title: How a Language Extends the Core
tour.description: Now watch the extension mechanism in action with a full language vertical. TypeScript is the ideal first example because it is itself a foundation that React, Angular, Vue, and Nuxt build on. Each file here extends its common/ counterpart via a 'related' edge: typescript/coding-style.md adds explicit public-API typing and Zod validation on top of the universal style rules, while patterns, security, and testing each layer language-specific guidance over the shared baseline from Step 2. This four-way split by concern (style / patterns / security / testing) is the template every language directory repeats.
tour.languageLesson: TypeScript generics express reusable, type-parameterized contracts — ApiResponse<T>, Repository<T>, useDebounce<T> — and the ruleset prefers `unknown` narrowing over `any`, string-literal unions over enums, and `z.infer` to derive types from Zod schemas so validation and types stay in lockstep.

tour.title: When a Language Overrides the Default
tour.description: Extension usually means 'add to' the common baseline, but the precedence model also allows a language to override it outright — the clearest illustration of the CSS-specificity analogy from Step 1. golang/coding-style.md deliberately diverges from the universal style guidance: where common/ favors generic immutability, Go mandates 'accept interfaces, return concrete structs', small 1-3 method interfaces, and %w error wrapping. The language-specific rule wins. Compare it side by side with common/coding-style.md to feel exactly where the override lands.
tour.languageLesson: Go idioms drive the override: gofmt/goimports formatting is non-negotiable, interfaces are defined by the consumer not the producer, and errors are wrapped with %w so callers can unwrap with errors.Is/errors.As — a pattern that has no analogue in the language-agnostic baseline.

tour.title: A Two-Level Stack
tour.description: Some rulesets sit two layers deep. React does not extend common/ directly for everything — it extends typescript/ (Step 4), which in turn extends common/ (Step 2). This is the catalogue's most tightly-coupled cluster: react/patterns.md and react/hooks.md cross-reference each other AND both reach back through typescript/patterns.md to common/patterns.md. The result is a layered stack — universal principles, then the TypeScript substrate, then React's component, RSC-boundary, and hooks-discipline specializations on top. Trace the 'related' edges here to see inheritance compose across three levels.
tour.languageLesson: Note the filename collision: react/hooks.md is about React's Rules of Hooks (useEffect, useMemo, the stale-closure trap), NOT the Claude Code PostToolUse/Stop hooks runtime that every other directory's hooks.md describes. The leading blockquote in the file disambiguates the two meanings.

tour.title: Breadth: Systems, Mobile, and Frameworks
tour.description: With the mechanism understood, sample how it scales across very different ecosystems. rust/patterns.md leans on traits, newtypes, and enum state machines for a systems language; swift/patterns.md applies protocol-oriented design, value types, and actors for Apple-platform mobile; and python/fastapi.md shows a third pattern — a framework-specific ruleset that extends python/patterns.md and python/security.md rather than common/ directly. Each is the same concern-split template from Step 4, re-skinned for its language's idioms, confirming the structure is uniform even as the content diverges wildly.

tour.title: The Web Domain's Extra Concerns
tour.description: Not every directory uses the standard four-file split. The web/ domain is a cross-cutting substrate beneath the UI frameworks that adds two concerns the others lack: web/design-quality.md enforces an anti-template policy with banned patterns and a per-component review checklist, and web/performance.md sets Core Web Vitals targets and bundle budgets. These sit alongside the usual style/security/testing files and feed React, Angular, Vue, and Nuxt — showing the catalogue extends its own template when a domain demands it.

tour.title: The Recurring Cross-Language Triad
tour.description: Close by zooming back out to the pattern that holds the whole catalogue together. Look at common/security.md, common/testing.md, and common/hooks.md as a triad — every single language directory carries its own security.md, testing.md, and hooks.md that extends these three. They are consistent cross-language checklists: security hardening, the testing/coverage contract, and the tooling-automation hooks that fire on edits. Whatever language you arrive in, you can predict these three files exist and know roughly what they enforce — the uniformity that makes the catalogue navigable is itself the final lesson.
